He collapsed in his bathtub in December 2023 and spent the next six months in the ICU, hospitals, and acute care facilities, then died in June 2024.
6) My niece's husband, an active and physically fit 57-year-old male, died suddenly of a heart attack in October 2024.
7) My brother-in-law, an engineer who had built his own house and remained physically active in his late 70s, suddenly developed cardiac issues and died two weeks later in hospice. He had received all the COVID shots and other recommended vaccines.
***In addition to the 7 fatalities listed above, another close neighbor, a healthy 16-year-old athlete, collapsed shortly after getting the first COVID shot. He was diagnosed with Pericarditis and, fortunately, survived.

Title: Comment from Almond, Margaret
Comment:
Our twin boys were typically developing through about 18 months of age. Around that time, we began noticing a significant regression. They lost speech, stopped making eye contact, and often appeared spaced out or disconnected from their surroundings.

As we pursued further medical evaluation and testing, we discovered their bodies were struggling in several areas. Their nervous systems appeared highly disregulated, and they began showing behaviors that later led to an autism diagnosis, such as hand flapping and sensory challenges.

Since then, we have been on a long journey trying to understand what happened and to support their healing. We have completed extensive testing and treatments, and I have included reports from some of that testing. This process has been emotionally and financially overwhelming for our family, and despite our efforts, our boys remain minimally verbal.

Through genetic testing, we also learned that both of our sons have MTHFR and COMT variants. These findings suggest that their bodies may process toxins, medications, and environmental exposures differently and may be more sensitive to certain medical interventions.

Because of our experience, I believe more research and precaution should be taken to ensure vaccines and other childhood medical interventions are as safe as possible for all children, especially those who may have underlying genetic vulnerabilities. I also believe genetic screening should be considered so that families and healthcare providers can make more informed, individualized decisions.

Our family continues to seek answers and support for our boys. We are advocating for continued research, transparency, and improvements in safety so that every child can receive care that is truly tailored to their individual biology.

I have documentation that I can provide to a secure platform. Including- Immunization records, Mitochondria Testing, Genetic Testing, Original IEP Evaluations, Bloodwork, Etc.

Let me know if you need additional information! Happy to help in any way we can!

Title: Comment from Triestman, Gary
Comment:
I will write about my good friend John.
He was a neighbor in the rooming house I live in for 8.5 years, by the time he left in 2022 he was 72 years old.
For virtually all the time he lived here, he was fairly active, would walk a mile a day, would come with me to Planet Fitness and lift weights and do cardio. Over the years he helped me build a 365 sq.ft. duck enclosure with concrete blocks and netting, he helped me build a raised platform/dock next to the house, and an 30' electrical line trench, among other house infrastructure projects.
I mention these things because it showed he had a basic constitution that maintained well and was stable. I was aware during that time he had mild high BP that he treated with meds, but otherwise healthy.
In 2020, he went to visit relatives, and I believe he may have caught covid, but he got over it and all was ok w/no lasting symptoms.
About July 2021, he decided to get the covid vaccine; I had told him he didn't need to because he already had covid and I was reading much about adverse effects of the vaccine. But he dismissed that concern as a fringe theory and that all the pundit doctors and media told everyone to get the shot. I went with him to CVS and got the shot, and he seemed ok initially thereafter; I vaguely remember him complaining about some minor aches/pains, and that his gout was acting up, and that he had bad bouts of acid reflux. But that seemed to get better and there weren't any more issues he mentioned. However over the next few months his physical demeanor, mental clarity and spryness were lessened over the next few months, he stopped doing his walks, and didn't want to go to the exercise. But he seemed ok otherwise.
Then he got a booster shot in October 2021, and later again complained about minor ailments. A month later I noticed he wasn't watching the financial shows he liked to watch in the morning. And he complained about dizziness, and I noticed he wasn't thinking as lucidly as he usually did. December I took his BP and was shocked to see it at around 220/120. He later made an appt with his doctor, but forgot that he had made it, and had to reschedule; on that reschedule it was only because I was driving him to the supermarket did he realize he also had the doctor's appt that day. The doctor observed the BP and upped his meds for that, and made several other appts for him for other testing. Later he missed going to them too.
At this point, I realized he was not in his right mind, and I tested his BP every other day, which stayed consistently high around 200/110,+-20points. I had to make sure he took his medication and got him a daily pill boxset, because when I asked him about he didn't know which bottle he had taken some from or how many or at all, I had to literally make sure he took them daily in the right amounts -- however this did not help his BP, which stayed around that value. I told him this was very serious, and he later called the ambulance, the EMT noted the 210/110 BP and called it serious enough to bring him to the local hospital, and thought at least it was good they would figure out what was wrong and give him a better medication regimen. But I was surprised to see him back home later that day; he said he had checked himself out of the hospital because it was too noisy; he had to sign a release form against their advice, and had a taxi bring him back.
I made another appointment with his doctor for him, and two weeks later brought him there; this time he had chronic edema and his legs had swollen up, the doctor upped his meds and added a diuretic.
But from there it went worse and he went down -- he was cold all the time and would sit directly in front of an electric heater for hours; he didn't know the date or where he was, he forgot he had relatives, he neglected to go to the bathroom, and had jars in his room filled instead.
Eventually local mental health dept and adult services was called, and they asked me to take another BP measurement for them. When they found out what it was (which it had consistently been for weeks), they called the ambulance, and I haven't seen him since. But I did find out he had failing kidneys and inefficient heart pumping, leading to the edema. He had to get dialysis.
I lost track of him since then, but since he did have family a few hundred miles away, they took over his medical proxy care.
I fully believe that the two vaccinations he got precipitated his rapid decline, and organ failure.
No one would know all this, unless I spoke up, so I am submitting this comment.
The ease at which John got injected with this compounded medication that effectively ended him, combined with the steady messages of confident assurances by official experts and health professionals, and the complete lack of any informed consent of actual dangers, made John confident and sure he was doing the right thing in getting this vaccination, even against my admittedly laymen's opinion.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

I am a person with long COVID deeply worried about recent harmful and unwarranted changes on vaccine policy at the CDC and the future prevention and treatment of Long COVID as a health condition. This is a vital issue to me because of the following below.

Today, I am writing to express grave concerns of the future of COVID vaccines and Long COVID.

Current vaccination rates for many infectious diseases are very low, and many US communities are directly affected by outbreaks of vaccine-preventable diseases such as measles, pertussis, and COVID. Many of these infections can cause serious and potentially lifelong consequences to people’s health. RFK Jr., Marty Makary, and Vinay Prasad have previously imposed new policies that limit access to COVID vaccinations. Restrictive policies create barriers to universal access to COVID vaccines, and are especially essential for children, pregnant people, older adults, and adults with underlying health conditions especially under the age of 65. These restrictions also create immense confusion among the public on the usefulness of COVID vaccines.

The ongoing spread of COVID infections will continue to drive an increase in the number of people who develop this serious condition. I urge you to take action, follow the evidence, and reinstate universal COVID vaccination recommendations for people of all ages, authorizing them twice a year (due to their rapid waning), for all COVID vaccine formulations. I ask this ACIP committee to vote for policies advancing universal access to vaccines and the prevention of Long COVID as well as to ensure that decisions are considered, evaluated, and concluded using open and transparent processes, all of which are hallmarks of democracy.

Comment:
Subject: Comment on Vaccine Safety Monitoring, Treatment Diversification, and Family Support

I am submitting this comment as the author of Cruel Diagnosis, that uses a pen name, and founder of a ministry dedicated to families navigating autism and neurodevelopmental delays. My family’s multi-generational experience with vaccine injury—spanning from my twins in 1989 to my loved one's regressions in 2017 and 2021—highlights critical gaps in our current public health framework that require urgent attention.

1. The Need for Enhanced Medical Oversight and Transparency
There is a profound lack of rigorous oversight regarding cumulative vaccine injury. My research indicates my children were injured in 1989, yet medical professionals advised me to ignore the symptoms. Decades later, my grandchild suffered a visible loss of skills after shots that he was aware of. I also learned in 2014 about my own lifetime of battles with CFS/ME, from the time I was administered a shot and a sugar cube in 1973. To prevent other families from being left "in the dark," we need:

Mandatory, Transparent Reporting Protocols: Consistent, nationwide application of injury reporting to ensure parental observations are documented and investigated.

Independent Monitoring: Rigorous study of long-term neurological outcomes following vaccine clusters.

2. Provision of Safe and Effective Family Resources
Families are too often left to navigate the "cruel diagnosis" of autism alone, frequently encountering dismissive rhetoric rather than real support in learning what to do for their child. The CDC must prioritize:

Validated Support Networks: Resources that respect and validate parental observations of regression.

Informed Consent Education: Comprehensive resources that address the reality of vaccine injury, allowing families to make medical decisions without fear of stigma.

3. Integration of Alternative, Non-Toxic Treatments and Facility Reform
Current medical models often focus on "management" rather than recovery, frequently relying on heavy pharmaceuticals to sedate residents in facilities. My ministry focuses on recovering children by addressing underlying comorbidities the standard model ignores. I urge the CDC to support:

Physiological Research: Focused investigation into gut dysbiosis, chronic inflammation, and neuroimmune delays as treatable components of autism.

Non-Toxic Therapeutic Pathways: A shift away from restrictive "nursing home" models—including those rebranded as "group homes"—which often utilize "zombifying" practices for ease of control. We must move toward holistic, biological interventions that promote genuine healing and skill regain.

Conclusion
Our most vulnerable—children, autistic adults, and the elderly—deserve to be liberated from barbaric "mental illness" practices and provided a legitimate path to recovery. I ask that you incorporate these calls for better oversight, safe family resources, and non-toxic treatment options into your future policy considerations.

Thank you for your time and for the opportunity to submit this testimony.

Dear ACIP Members,

I am an unvaccinated individual from Poland. Since spring 2021, I have experienced a clear and repeatable pattern: even brief close contact (as little as 5 minutes) with vaccinated individuals consistently triggers in me HSV-1 reactivation. Longer contact (over 15–30 minutes) leads to a broader set of symptoms within hours to days. The most reliable sign is herpes simplex virus reactivation, usually on the face but also along nerve pathways. Other symptoms include:
•severe, prolonged headaches (often lasting >10 days, sometimes migraine-like with photophobia, phonophobia and nausea – I cannot tolerate music or loud sounds),
•unilateral sciatica and sacral nerve dysfunction (incontinence, numbness),
•chronic swelling and non-healing skin lesions (scabs) on the left lower leg and foot.
These symptoms are strongly asymmetric: the left leg has always been the worst affected. The right leg was also very swollen and scabbed for over 30 months but has mostly recovered. The left leg remains swollen and scabbed to this day.
Even indirect exposure (e.g., walking through a room where a vaccinated family member had spent several hours earlier) can cause headache and HSV reactivation. Herpes outbreaks are often biphasic: the initial lesion heals after 10–14 days, but a second wave appears shortly after without any obvious new trigger.
Strict isolation (no close contact with vaccinated individuals for months) dramatically reduces HSV reactivations, but does not eliminate all symptoms. Chronic headache, mild swelling and leg issues persist even during long periods of isolation.
I have pre-existing conditions (celiac disease, hyper-reactive mast cells, childhood nerve damage in the lumbar/sacral region from medical procedures, and a severe knee injury at age ~30) that likely increase my sensitivity.
Despite thousands of similar anecdotal reports worldwide, shedding and its effects on unvaccinated individuals remain almost completely unstudied and unacknowledged.

I respectfully ask the Committee to consider these real-world experiences when evaluating long-term vaccine safety, especially for sensitive individuals and the unvaccinated population.

Thank you for your time and attention.

Title: Comment from Morefield, Joanna

Fast forward nearly 40 years: I went to live with my 84 year old mother during Covid shutdowns in October of 2020. As soon as the vaccines were released to be given to senior citizens, my mother took two Moderna mRNA injectables about 6 weeks apart. As the goalposts and recommendations changed, she also received a third shot, a booster, in December of 2021. Title: Comment from Lewis , Amber
Comment:
I am submitting this comment to urge greater recognition, research, and physician education regarding Long COVID.

I developed Long COVID in 2023 after my third COVID-19 infection. At the time, I had received two vaccinations and a booster, and I was a healthy 39-year-old with no significant medical issues. The illness completely changed my life.

After the infection, I began experiencing a wide range of debilitating symptoms that I had never had before. These included severe dizziness, tachycardia, panic attacks, extreme anxiety, rashes, food reactions to foods I had always tolerated, chronic dry eyes, mental fatigue, nausea, insomnia, and severe eye strain that made it difficult to look at screens or drive. The dizziness and visual disturbances became so severe that I temporarily lost the ability to drive.

The psychological effects were also profound. The combination of neurological symptoms, sleep deprivation, and constant physical distress led to periods of intense anxiety and suicidal thoughts. It is difficult to describe the level of suffering this condition can cause.

When I sought medical care, I quickly discovered that most physicians are not trained to recognize or treat Long COVID. My primary care doctor had no guidance to offer. Basic laboratory tests were often the only option presented, yet they were extremely expensive even with insurance and rarely provided any meaningful answers. Like many patients, I was left navigating a complex and frightening illness largely on my own.

My experience is not unique. Millions of Americans are living with Long COVID, many of whom were previously healthy and active members of the workforce. The lack of clinical education, standardized diagnostic pathways, and accessible research-backed treatments leaves patients without the care they urgently need.

For these reasons, I strongly urge regulators and policymakers to:

• Increase federal funding for Long COVID research
• Support development of clinical guidelines for diagnosis and treatment
• Require physician education and training on Long COVID and post-viral syndromes
• Improve access to affordable testing and multidisciplinary care for patients

Long COVID is real, disabling, and affecting a significant number of Americans. Patients should not have to fight to be believed or to access knowledgeable medical care. Increased research and physician education are critical to ensuring that people suffering from this condition receive the care and understanding they deserve.

Title: Comment from Skovbo Soested, Tina
Comment:
Public Comment – COVID-19 Vaccine Injury Testimony

Thank you to CDC and ACIP for addressing vaccine injuries and for opening this important hearing. Acknowledging and taking the experiences of injured patients seriously is essential not only for helping those affected — public trust in health authorities and vaccination programs depends on it.

On July 1, 2021, I received the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine in Denmark. Within 5–10 minutes, I developed acute neurological symptoms, including paresthesia on the right side of my head and face radiating into my arm and hand. Despite these symptoms, I was sent home without proper medical evaluation or documentation.

Shortly after leaving the vaccination center, while driving home in traffic, I experienced severe dizziness and near-syncope behind the wheel — creating a potentially life-threatening situation for myself and others. When I contacted my doctor’s emergency line, I was refused an examination, and the adverse reaction was never reported to the vaccine safety monitoring system — undermining the very surveillance systems meant to protect patients.

Since that day, I have developed clinically documented chronic conditions consistent with Post-Acute Vaccine Syndrome (PAVS), including ME/CFS-like illness, post-immun autonomic dysfunction (PAIS), and POTS-like dysautonomia with multifocal ventricular arrhythmias originating from four distinct areas of my heart, along with chronic right-sided facial and head paresthesia and persistent headaches. Nearly five years later, these conditions continue to cause significant and lasting disability.

Before vaccination, I was healthy and working full-time (37 hours per week). Today, because of these conditions, I can work only about 9 hours per week.

Patients who experience serious reactions should never be left to navigate this alone, especially when the intervention was recommended by government and public health authorities.

I urge CDC and ACIP to investigate vaccine injuries thoroughly, establish specialized clinical programs for affected patients, and ensure that those harmed are properly recognized, studied, and supported. Concrete action is needed to ensure that this never happens again. A transparent and independent reassessment of the safety of mRNA vaccines is urgently needed. In the meantime, I call for an immediate suspension of mRNA vaccines until their safety has been fully and transparently reassessed.

The lives of COVID-19 vaccine-injured patients matter. We deserve recognition, research, and care. Ignoring those who were harmed will only erode trust in public health — addressing it openly is the only way to restore it.

— Tina, Denmark

Title: Comment from AuBuchon, David
Comment:
Universal hepatitis B vaccination may have increased overall infections (relative to targeted vaccination) through risk compensation:

See attached letter. A severe issue that further weighs against the old policy of universal hepatitis B vaccination went overlooked during the last meeting. It has relevance both in terms of providing a bulwark against future ACIP committees misguidedly reversing the vote, and in providing an example of an issue that might have relevance to recommendations regarding other vaccines. The issue in question is risk compensation, also known as the Peltzman effect. Policy affects behavior. When people perceive themselves to be at decreased risk of an adverse outcome (for example owing to implementation of a protective policy), they are likely to eschew other protective measures. This has been discussed in various contexts such as oral pre-exposure prophylaxis of HIV, COVID-19 vaccination, face mask use, and HPV vaccination (1-7). One paper even suggests that a hypothetical HIV vaccine could under certain circumstances have a net deleterious effect on public health in the long-term owing to behavioral changes privileging the spread of vaccine-resistant strains (8). In this brief letter I describe the multifaceted ways this may have happened with hepatitis B vaccination, most notably through a reduction in perceived need for maternal testing. Sensitivity analysis suggests that it is very well within the range of possibilities that universal vaccination has led to a net increase in hepatitis B infections, relative to the counterfactual of having remained with targeted vaccination. My recommendations to ACIP are as follows:

1. ACIP should recommend that research on risk compensation be carried out, especially while there is still an adequate living memory among people who lived under the universal hepatitis B recommendation.
2. ACIP should also consider the relevance of risk compensation to other vaccine recommendations currently on the books or in the pipeline.

Sincerely,
David AuBuchon

Title: Comment from Pettit, Natasha
Comment:
As an infectious diseases medical professional, I urge ACIP to once again serve as a source of credible, evidence-based information to inform vaccine coverage. Current members lacking vaccine expertise and appointed without transparency should be dismissed, and new members with the necessary qualifications should be appointed per the ACIP charter and federal law. Until ACIP is trustworthy and scientifically sound, it should not meet.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

Fast foward to today Guillain-Barré syndrome affects my ability to walk and perform everyday activities. Due to the nerve damage and muscle weakness associated with Guillain-Barré syndrome, I experience significant limitations in my mobility. Walking for extended distances is difficult because my legs fatigue quickly, and I often experience weakness, instability, and poor balance. These symptoms make it challenging to maintain a normal walking pace and increase my risk of falling. Even while using a cane or walker. I must frequently stop and rest when walking, and I cannot reliably walk long distances without experiencing substantial discomfort, weakness and shortness of breath. Hence why I have use a my personal mobile electric scooter to navigate around or an electric scooter provided by the stores. Even short distances can become exhausting, particularly when there are inclines, uneven surfaces, or periods of standing involved.

The condition also causes lingering nerve-related symptoms such as tingling, numbness, electric shocks and reduced coordination in my lower extremities. These issues further affect my walking ability and make navigating stairs, curbs, and crowded areas more difficult and unsafe.

Because of these ongoing physical limitations, my mobility is significantly restricted compared to a healthy individual. My condition requires me to carefully manage my physical activity and often rely on assistance, accommodations, or mobility aids in order to move safely and avoid worsening symptoms.

These walking limitations are a direct result of my Guillain-Barré syndrome and have a substantial impact on my daily functioning and independence. For this i was forced to retire and possibly never be able to work again!

I urge the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices to consider patient perspectives and real-world experiences in its deliberations. Our presence here reflects lived suffering, unmet needs, and the importance of inclusive dialogue. We do exist. Please consider how policies on vaccine recommendation guidelines, post-adverse event care, and research priorities affect real people every day.

Thank you.

Title: Comment from Barcavage, Shaun
Comment:
I am a nurse practitioner who has spent much of my career working in infectious disease and clinical research.

In 2021 I received the COVID-19 vaccine while working in healthcare. After my first dose I experienced neurological symptoms that were unusual for me. They were concerning enough that I questioned whether something wasn’t right, but the guidance at the time was to proceed with the second dose. Hospitals were moving toward vaccine mandates, and like many healthcare workers I felt pressure to comply.

After the second dose, everything changed.

Within days I developed severe tinnitus, neurological symptoms, cardiac rhythm disturbances, neuropathic pain, and profound insomnia. What began as an acute reaction quickly became a cascading medical situation that altered my life and my health.

Before this I was healthy and working full-time in clinical research. Since then I have required cardiac procedures for arrhythmias and continue to live with neurological and autonomic symptoms years later.

I have undergone treatments such as IVIg and plasma exchange. Most of that only happened because I pushed for it myself. As both a clinician and a patient, I spent years researching, advocating, and trying to piece together care for a condition that medicine does not yet have a clear framework for.

Many patients do not have that ability. They are simply left to suffer.

What has been most difficult is not only the illness itself, but what happens after.

As a clinician, I assumed that if serious adverse reactions occurred there would be a system designed to investigate them, understand them, and help the people affected. Instead, many patients quickly discover there is no clear pathway.

Doctors have little guidance on how to recognize or treat post-vaccination syndromes. Research into these conditions has been minimal. Patients whose symptoms do not fit a recognized category are often dismissed or left to navigate the medical system on their own.

There has also been an atmosphere around this topic that makes open discussion difficult. Patients who try to talk about vaccine injury often encounter dismissal, reputational risk, or pressure to stay quiet because acknowledging harm is seen as threatening to public health messaging. That kind of environment discourages honest scientific inquiry.

Another issue that deserves attention is the federal compensation system. The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program was supposed to provide a safety net for people harmed by pandemic countermeasures. In reality, many patients find it extremely difficult to access and limited in what it provides. For people whose health and livelihoods have been severely affected, it often feels less like a safety net and more like a closed door.

I want to be clear about something: the people experiencing these injuries are not anti-vaccine activists. Many of us were healthcare professionals or individuals who stepped forward and did exactly what we were asked to do during a public health emergency.

We trusted the system.

What has been difficult to accept is how many of those same people now feel abandoned by it.

When a medical intervention is deployed on this scale, society has a responsibility not only to promote its benefits but also to study and care for the people who experience serious adverse outcomes.

Right now there are many patients living with complex post-vaccination illnesses who feel stranded between medicine, public health institutions, and a compensation system that does not function the way people expected.

At a minimum there needs to be:

• serious research into post-vaccination syndromes
• clinical guidance so physicians know how to recognize and treat them
• transparent investigation of adverse events
• and a compensation system that actually works for the people it was created to help

Patients like me are not entries in a safety database. Our lives changed overnight.

We deserve to be studied, to be listened to, and to be helped.

Thank you.

Title: Comment from SEYMOUR, STEPHANIE
Comment: My son was injured from the Hep B vax on his day of birth. Following the injection, he had a 103 fever. They fumbled with their tools and were adamant that my son did not have a seizure because there was no fever. We demanded they take us to the hospital and they agreed, but with no lights or sirens. She immediately put him under a warming lamp with blankets and put on a cpap machine. We were admitted and they would not allow me to feed him until he had another seizure. On day 10 I said no more and we left. We had his lab work reviewed by a family friend who is an infectious disease doctor and felt it was absurd what they were doing. He was an incredibly difficult baby, screaming all the time unless sleeping, eating, or us implementing "The Happiest Baby on the Block 5's," which is a marvelous book. Title: Comment from Hamilton, AnnMarie
Comment:
Multiple independent analyses now make it abundantly clear that the mRNA COVID-19 vaccines have failed to meet basic standards of safety. Data from the U.S. *Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS)* show over **1.6 million adverse reactions** and nearly **39,000 deaths** associated with COVID-19 vaccination through October 2025—**more than triple** all other vaccine-related deaths combined since 1990 (*VAERSAnalysis.info, 2025*). Even these are only the detectable signals in a profoundly underreported system. Peer-reviewed and regulatory reviews estimate VAERS captures **as few as 1 in 31 serious adverse events**, according to Hulscher et al. (2025, *Science, Public Health Policy and the Law*), implying that total injuries and fatalities could exceed **half a million American deaths** related to these products.

Autopsy-confirmed evidence strengthens this conclusion. The 2024 *IPAK systematic review* of 325 post-vaccine autopsies found that **73.9% of deaths were directly caused or significantly contributed to by the vaccine**, with predominant lesions in the cardiovascular and hematologic systems—myocarditis, pulmonary embolism, and sudden cardiac arrest. Complementary work by Jessica Rose (2025) revealed an eighteen-fold increase in reported autopsy cases compared with influenza vaccines, yet far fewer autopsies performed as a proportion of reported deaths, suggesting systemic suppression of forensic follow‑up.

Independent risk‑benefit assessments are now unequivocal: The rate of death and serious illness following these injections dwarfs any measurable benefit—particularly when almost 90% of Americans already possess natural immunity. As early as 2023, Gøtzsche and Demasi’s meta‑review documented statistically significant excesses of serious adverse events in the pivotal mRNA trials themselves, hidden behind manipulated denominators and early unblinding.

Taken together, this body of evidence far exceeds the FDA’s **Class I recall threshold**—indicating “reasonable probability of causing serious injury or death.” Continuing to market these products without halting distribution, auditing manufacturer data, and initiating full criminal and regulatory inquiry represents not medicine but malfeasance. Immediate market withdrawal is not only justified—it is ethically obligatory.

Sources:

1.VAERS Analysis (Nov 29 2025).
“VAERS Summary for COVID 19 Vaccines through 10/31/2025.”
VAERS Analysis Info.
https://vaersanalysis.info/2025/11/29/vaers-summary-for-covid-19-vaccines-through-10-31-2025/

2.Hulscher N., Bowden M.T., McCullough P.A. (2025).

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment:
Docket: CDC-2026-0199

Public Comment for March 18 to 19 CDC ACIP Committee Meeting

To Members of the CDC ACIP committee:

I have no conflicts of interest to disclose.

I am a citizen that's deeply worried about recent harmful and unwarranted changes on vaccine policy at the CDC and the future prevention and treatment of Long COVID as a health condition. This is a vital issue to me because of the following below.

Today, I am writing to express grave concerns of the future of COVID vaccines and Long COVID.

Current vaccination rates for many infectious diseases are very low, and many US communities are directly affected by outbreaks of vaccine-preventable diseases such as measles, pertussis, and COVID. Many of these infections can cause serious and potentially lifelong consequences to people’s health. Children in 2026 should not die or become disabled from infectious diseases because they lack access to affordable and effective vaccines. Long COVID also continues to affect millions of Americans caused by a COVID infection. It continues to be debilitating condition that affects people’s health.

RFK Jr., Marty Makary, and Vinay Prasad have previously imposed new policies that limit access to COVID vaccinations. Creating such barriers is reckless and will without doubt harm the public’s health. Updated COVID vaccines must remain universally accessible and widely available at no cost to everyone. The scientific evidence is irrefutable, so it not only endangers Americans’ health but also is a waste of precious resources and time to require additional efficacy studies, which would only serve to sow doubt in vaccines. Receiving a COVID vaccine within the last year reduces the risk of symptoms, severe disease, disability, and death for people of all ages, including previously healthy people. Restrictive policies create barriers to universal access to COVID vaccines, and are especially essential for children, pregnant people, older adults, and adults with underlying health conditions especially under the age of 65. These restrictions also create immense confusion among the public on the usefulness of COVID vaccines.

Long COVID is a severe health condition caused by a COVID infection. Long COVID is a debilitating condition that affects millions of Americans. The ongoing spread of COVID infections will continue to drive an increase in the number of people who develop this serious condition. We need science-based experts to provide oversight on the prevention and treatment of Long COVID. It is very important to develop effective treatments to address this condition.

I urge you to take action, follow the evidence, and reinstate universal COVID vaccination recommendations for people of all ages, authorizing them twice a year (due to their rapid waning), for all COVID vaccine formulations. I urge you to ensure science-based experts oversee the future direction of addressing Long COVID either by preventing it or developing effective treatments. I ask this ACIP committee to vote for policies advancing universal access to vaccines and the prevention of Long COVID as well as to ensure that decisions are considered, evaluated, and concluded using open and transparent processes, all of which are hallmarks of democracy.

Thank you for your consideration.

Title: Comment from Lemon, Katherine
Comment:
As a pediatric infectious diseases medical professional, I urge ACIP to once again serve as a source of credible, evidence-based information to inform vaccine coverage. Current members lacking vaccine expertise and appointed without transparency should be dismissed, and new members with the necessary qualifications should be appointed per the ACIP charter and federal law. Until ACIP is trustworthy and scientifically sound, it should not meet.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

Title: Comment from Peugh, Isaac
Comment:
I.P. (23 y.o. male at time of vaccine)
BLUF: Within 7 months of receiving the Pfizer COVID vaccine at the beginning of Army training, I developed increasing debilitating arthritic pain which was later diagnosed to be Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS).
I enlisted in the US Army in October to 2021 and received the Pfizer COVID vaccine after arriving at boot camp. Over the course of the next 7 months (October 2021-April 2022) of my Infantry One Station Unit Training (OSUT) I developed increasingly intense inflammation.
At the beginning of OSUT (October 2021), I was running the maximum score on the 2-mile run (6:15/min/mile pace) on the Army Physical Fitness Test. The arthritic pain first presented as a deep aching in my Sacroiliac (SI) joints and then progressed to my upper back and neck. The ache in my neck and upper back was so severe in the mornings that it took significant effort to physically raise myself from a lying position to upright. The inflammation kept me from turning my head. The SI joint pain was so severe my fellow soldiers noted my change in gait to that of an “old man”. As an athletic 23-year-old, I was demoralized by the drop in my physical performance on account of my symptoms. 7 months after receiving the vaccine (April 2022), as the effects of the symptoms peaked, I was unable to break a 9 min/mile pace.

In May 2022, I received an MRI and Bloodwork. I was initially diagnosed with high grade stress fractures in my pelvis. However, after 8 months (May 2022-January 2023) of deliberate rest, which did not alleviate the symptoms, I was recommended to a Rheumatologist in January of 2023. Based on the original May 2022 MRI and new bloodwork, he diagnosed me with Ankylosing Spondylitis. The blood results indicated high C-Reactive Protein, Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate levels, and other inflammatory markers - but curiously showed I was negative for the HLA-B27 gene.

HLA-B27 is present in 90% of patients diagnosed with AS. I do not have any family members with this disease. Because my case is HLA-B27 negative and the symptoms began soon after receiving the Pfizer COVID-19 vaccine in October 2021, it raises the high possibility that the immune response to vaccination triggered the onset of my Ankylosing Spondylitis.

What began as a promising start to my Army career and desire to serve has been fundamentally altered by this life-changing diagnosis. Now, I strive to regain the mobility I have lost and find the endurance to bear this chronic inflammation for the rest of my life. I would not wish the pain and debilitation I have experienced since October of 2021 on anyone.

Respectfully,
IP

PS:

Title: Comment from Silverman, Elaine
Comment:
To the members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP)

I am a General Internal Medicine Physician who has been practicing in Primary Care for over 30 years. I also hold a Masters of Public Health and have worked in health services research. Additionally, I have 2 sons in their 20's who are thankfully healthy and productive young men, and I am quite thankful I was able to raise them in a country that has historically been the home of ground breaking vaccine discovery and, until recently, has had a government that has supported and advocated for broad access to these life-saving preventative medicines.

Unfortunately the U.S. vaccine development and regulatory infrastructure faces unprecedented challenges that threaten both
innovation and public health preparedness. Recent policy changes have disrupted decades of scientific progress
while undermining the evidence-based frameworks that have guided vaccine recommendations since the 1960s.
Altering the U.S. vaccine schedule to mirror Denmark’s model is not an appropriate or evidence-based
comparison. Denmark has a population roughly equivalent to New York State, operates under a universal health coverage or
consistent access to care, and encompasses a highly diverse population with distinct epidemiologic patterns and
demographic considerations. These substantial differences necessitate vaccination policies specifically designed
for the U.S. public health landscape. Decisions regarding vaccine schedules should therefore be grounded in
U.S.-specific epidemiology, access realities, and population health needs rather than arbitrarily aligning with
another country’s program.
The Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP), which has existed since 1964 to advise on vaccine
use in the U.S. civilian population, has experienced a systematic dismantling of its evidence-based processes. AllACIP committee members were summarily removed on June 9, 2025, and replaced with members having
strong ties to the anti-vaccine movement.
The ACIP's decision-making framework represents decades of methodological refinement:
• The GRADE (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation) framework,
formally adopted in 2010, ensures vaccine recommendations are based on systematic evidence review
with transparent evaluation of evidence quality.
• The Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework, adopted in 2018, adds structured evaluation of
implementation factors, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives, and equity.
These frameworks were specifically designed to improve transparency and consistency following the 2008-2011
Institute of Medicine reports calling for stronger clinical guideline development methods.
Current ACIP discussions include updates on workgroups and potential votes on recommendations regarding
COVID-19 vaccine injuries and Long-COVID methodology. ACIP should reaffirm that its recommendation
methodology remains grounded in the formal GRADE and EtR frameworks, rejecting any methodology changes
that would permit recommendations without documented evidence assessment and completed EtR review. Any
reconsideration of methodology must be fully documented in public materials with a clear explanation of how
revised processes will maintain evidence-based standards.
Vaccine safety surveillance data is being altered or withheld without explanation, while funding for vaccine
confidence research has been cut.
1 Simultaneously, efforts to revive debunked claims linking vaccines to autism
are being funded, contradicting decades of scientific evidence. Growing attempts to access personally
identifiable health data without transparency or stakeholder engagement are a breach of public trust.
The convergence of these changes—defunded research, altered regulatory processes, dismantled advisory
structures, and compromised data systems—represents a departure from the evidence-based, transparent
decision-making that has characterized U.S. vaccine policy and enabled decades of life-saving innovation.
Additionally, the FDA has experienced significant workforce reductions that will delay new product
development and potentially compromise safety inspections. More concerning are recent procedural changes
that bypass established regulatory processes:
These actions undermine regulatory consistency, transparency, and the involvement of expert decisionmakers—all essential conditions for public trust.
I respectfully asks that the CDC postpone this meeting until it can ensurethe American Public that all its decisions are transparent and consistently following evidenced based practices.
I ask as a physician, and as a mother, who has hopes for seeing my future grandchildren grow up with as much opportunity to be healthy as my children had,

Thank you.
Elaine Silverman, MD, MPH, FACP

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

My neighbor developed temporary paralysis, severe chest pain, and difficulty breathing within hours of receiving his dose. One of my clients experienced anaphylaxis after her second Moderna vaccination and was later found to have developed a blood clot following the first.

My friend who received one AstraZeneca dose followed by Pfizer was later diagnosed with Sjögren’s syndrome and now lives with tremors. My father developed psoriatic arthritis after Pfizer despite having no history of autoimmune disease. My sister experienced severe menstrual disruption after vaccination, later developed alopecia areata and lost all of her hair, and is currently being investigated for systemic lupus erythematosus. For the people affected, these were not temporary reactions. These were life-changing health conditions. Scientific evidence documenting adverse events following vaccination has also appeared in the medical literature. Cases of myocarditis and pericarditis following mRNA vaccination were identified, particularly in adolescent and young adult males. Myocarditis is inflammation of the heart muscle and represents injury to the myocardium. Individuals diagnosed with myocarditis are commonly advised to restrict strenuous activity and require cardiology follow-up due to potential complications including arrhythmias and cardiac dysfunction. The association between mRNA vaccination and myocarditis has been documented in cardiology research including work published in Circulation by Bozkurt and colleagues and population analyses in JAMA Cardiology and Nature Medicine examining myocarditis risk after mRNA vaccination.

Reports include small fiber neuropathy described by Primicerio and colleagues, postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome reported in studies published in Nature Cardiovascular Research and Heart Rhythm, alopecia areata reviewed by Zhu and colleagues, inflammatory arthritis described by Liu and colleagues, and autoimmune conditions including systemic lupus erythematosus summarized in case literature by Khanna and colleagues. Research examining immune-mediated inflammatory responses following vaccination has also appeared in journals including JAMA, Nature Medicine, and Circulation.

Title: Comment from American College of Nurse-Midwives
Comment:
Docket No. CDC-2026-0199

Recommendation to the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP)
COVID-19 Vaccination During Pregnancy

Submitted by the American College of Nurse-Midwives (ACNM)
March 2026

The American College of Nurse-Midwives (ACNM) appreciates the opportunity to provide recommendations to the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) regarding COVID-19 vaccination during pregnancy. ACNM represents certified nurse-midwives and certified midwives who provide reproductive and maternity care across the United States and play an important role in counseling pregnant individuals about evidence-based preventive care, including maternal immunizations.

ACNM strongly supports continued federal recommendations for COVID-19 vaccination during pregnancy. Extensive evidence demonstrates that vaccination during pregnancy is safe and provides meaningful benefits for both pregnant individuals and their infants. Clear federal guidance is essential to support clinicians in counseling patients and ensuring access to preventive care.

Risk of COVID-19 During Pregnancy

Pregnancy is associated with increased risk of severe illness from COVID-19 compared with nonpregnant individuals of reproductive age. National surveillance data show that pregnant individuals with symptomatic infection are more likely to require hospitalization, intensive care, mechanical ventilation, or extracorporeal membrane oxygenation.¹

COVID-19 infection during pregnancy has also been associated with adverse obstetric outcomes. A large living systematic review found increased risks of preterm birth, hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, and neonatal intensive care unit admission among infected pregnant individuals.²

Infants younger than six months are also vulnerable to severe COVID-19 disease yet remain ineligible for vaccination, making maternal immunization an important strategy to protect both pregnant individuals and their infants.

Effectiveness of Maternal Vaccination

COVID-19 vaccination during pregnancy provides direct protection to the pregnant individual and indirect protection to the infant through transplacental transfer of maternal antibodies. Studies demonstrate robust maternal immune responses following vaccination and efficient transfer of SARS-CoV-2–specific IgG antibodies across the placenta.³

A multicenter U.S. case-control study found that maternal vaccination during pregnancy reduced the risk of COVID-19–associated hospitalization among infants younger than six months by approximately 52%.⁴ CDC analyses reported similar findings, with maternal vaccination reducing infant hospitalization risk by 35% overall and 54% among infants younger than three months.⁵

Safety of COVID-19 Vaccination in Pregnancy

Extensive evidence supports the safety of COVID-19 vaccination during pregnancy. Data from large observational cohorts, pregnancy registries, and national vaccine safety monitoring systems show no increased risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes associated with vaccination.⁶

Early CDC v-safe pregnancy registry data found that rates of miscarriage, preterm birth, and congenital anomalies among vaccinated individuals were comparable to background rates.⁷ Subsequent systematic reviews involving hundreds of thousands of vaccinated pregnant individuals similarly found no increased risk of miscarriage, congenital anomalies, preterm birth, or other adverse maternal or neonatal outcomes.⁸

Importance of Clear Federal Guidance

Clinician recommendation is one of the strongest predictors of vaccine uptake during pregnancy. Clear and consistent national recommendations allow clinicians—including midwives, obstetricians, family physicians, and pediatricians—to provide confident counseling and support informed decision-making.

Unclear federal messaging regarding COVID-19 vaccination in pregnancy may contribute to lower vaccine uptake and increased preventable morbidity among pregnant individuals and infants.

Recommendations

Based on the current evidence, ACNM respectfully recommends that ACIP:

• Continue recommending COVID-19 vaccination for individuals who are pregnant.
• Affirm that COVID-19 vaccines may be administered during any trimester of pregnancy.
• Recognize maternal vaccination as an important strategy for protecting infants through passive antibody transfer.
• Maintain clear federal recommendations aligned with major professional organizations.
• Support continued research and surveillance related to maternal immunization.

Conclusion

COVID-19 vaccination during pregnancy is supported by extensive evidence demonstrating safety and clinical benefit. Vaccination protects pregnant individuals from severe illness and provides passive immunity to infants during the early months of life.

ACNM urges ACIP to maintain clear and consistent recommendations supporting COVID-19 vaccination during pregnancy to protect maternal and infant health.

Title: Comment from Anonymous

This is simply my informal feedback/input re. side-effects/injuries/consequences of some covid-19 vaccines. I will tell my own personal experience and I will tell some anecdotes of some of my patients (I work in healthcare as a chiropractor and see approximately 80 patient visits per week (each year I see approximately 500 unique/individual patients).

I received the Johnson/Jahnnsen vaccine in Septemberr 2021, because it was mandated in my state and I would have lost my license to practice if I did not comply. About 1-2 weeks after being vaccinated, I developed a sensitive crust-like layer inside my nose (lining the nasal mucosa, bilaterally). This was very unusual for me as I had not felt anything like this for many years, perhaps ever. This crust was accompanied by a sensitivity or inflammation that caused pain if I touched the lower half of my nose. In other words, if I were to nose my nose and move the cartilage part to the left and right, there was substantial pain! I thought, "how strange" maybe this is dried mucous aka "snot". So, I tried to blow my nose which resulted in a nosebleed. This crust and accompanying inflammation remained for about 90 days!!! I had to be careful when dressing or playing with my dog or even when sleeping and make sure that my nose never got bumped or pushed in or pushed from side to side because doing so was very painful and would sometime cause bleeding. After 90 days, it finally resolved. I have worked in healthcare for 30 years and I am 100% certain that this strange condition was related to the vaccine I received. I filed a report in the vaers system. That is my story.

Now I will briefly describe a handful of anecdotes of my own patients:

1-Healthy 60 year old male on no meds received one of the mRNA vaccines in approximately October 2021. He died a few weeks later, in his bed, apparently while sleeping. There was no autopsy but the cause of death was officially determined to be diabetic ketoacidosis. I am dubious re this stated cause of death because I knew that this patient was NOT diabetic. He previously was a type 2 diabetic and he did have a prescription for metformin, but he no longer took it after going on a low carb diet approximately 1-2 years prior and losing weight and getting his HA1c below 6.0% Prior to this he was active, and healthy. No family member suspected the vaccine, no doctor mentioned this as a possibility. To my knowledge, no vaers report was ever filed.

2-Robust, active male patient in his 70s, no medications received mRNA vaccine in spring 2021 after first dose he developed severe pain and weakness and nearly lost his ability to walk. I saw him in my office and stated that my best guess was that the symptoms were "most likely" a result of the vaccine. The condition was very similar to a guillane-barre type syndrome or some type of myelitis. I suggested a neuro consult. He went to a neurologist and then a rheumatologist and was diagnosed with polymyalgia rheumatica. I told him that I did not agree with that Dx. Over several months he slowly got better and recovered about 90% in 6 months. Then, he received his second dose of the same vaccine and the same thing happened once again!! This time was not as bad (of note is that he was now on anti-inflammatories (prednisone, et al) and some type of immunotherapy for his alleged polymyalgia). To my knowledge, he never fully recovered and remained on medications. No vaers report was ever filed and the patient never saw me again possibly because he believed I was somewhat off-base with my opinion. Oh well.

3-Healthy/active Female patient in her 40s. Received mRNA vaccine in summer of 2021 because her employer (a healthcare facility) mandated it as a condition of employment. A few weeks later, while traveling, she developed severe chest pain and went to nearby ER. After a workup, she was diagnosed with multiple coronary clots and also clots in her lungs too. She received open heart surgery and had bypass surgeries performed. She nearly died in the hospital, but slowly recovered and became strong enough to go home. She was prescribed a blood thinner and returned home. No other meds were given and she did not change her lifestyle (because it was already very healthful). Future evaluations revealed that she had zero atherosclerosis and zero evidence of arterial disease. So, the acute, multiple clots (which occurred shortly after the vaccine) to this day, are the only objective cardio findings that she has ever had. No vaers report was filed.

These stories are told to the best of my memory. I do not have their charts in front of me. I admit that i may have some smaller details wrong but my details are pretty close I believe. The general facts are pretty accurate. Of note is that the above are just a sampling from my practice, there are more stories than what is above.

Thank you for reading.
Dr. J.C. (I wish to remain anonymous)

Title: Comment from Kressel, Amy
Comment:
As an infectious diseases medical professional, I urge ACIP to once again serve as a source of credible, evidence-based information to inform vaccine coverage. Current members lacking vaccine expertise and appointed without transparency should be dismissed, and new members with the necessary qualifications should be appointed per the ACIP charter and federal law. Until ACIP is trustworthy and scientifically sound, it should not meet.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. MERELY DOWNGRADING VACCINE RECOMMENDATIONS TO MATCH THE LOWEST COMMON DENOMINATOR OF INTERNATIONAL SCHEDULES IS NOT GOLD-STANDARD SCIENCE. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals. FURTHERMORE, ALL STUDIES LOOKING AT VACCINE INJURY SHOULD BE CONSIDERED, AND VACCINE INJURY NEEDS TO BE EXAMINED IN THE CONTEXT OF INJURY FROM THE DISEASE THE VACCINE IS PREVENTING. SPECIFICALLY, THERE IS ONE OUTLIER STUDY SHOWING INCREASED RISK OF FETAL LOSS WHEN PREGNANT WOMEN ARE GIVEN mRNA COVID VACCINE IN THE 1ST TRIMESTER ONLY. THIS SAME STUDY SHOWED DECREASED RISK OF FETAL LOSS WHEN mRNA COVID VACCINE WAS GIVEN PREPARTUM OR DURING THE 2ND AND 3RD TRIMESTERS. JUST AS IMPORTANT, OTHER LARGE STUDIES DONE BOTH BEFORE AND AFTER THIS OUTLIER STUDY SHOWED THAT COVID VACCINES ARE SAFE IN PREGNANT WOMEN, AND IT IS WELL-ESTABLISHED THAT PREGNANT WOMEN ARE AT INCREASED RISK OF SEVERE COVID. IF CONCERNS REMAIN ABOUT A SMALL RISK OF COVID VACCINES IN THE 1ST TRIMESTER, THEN ACIP SHOULD CONSIDER RECOMMENDING THAT ALL WOMEN CONSIDERING PREGNANCY SHOULD RECEIVE COVID VACCINE BEFORE PREGNANCY, SIMILAR TO RECOMMENDATIONS FOR FOLATE FOR ALL WOMEN CONSIDERING PREGNANCY.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

May God grant us all good health.

Title: Comment from Strength, Rachel
Comment:
As an infectious diseases medical professional, I urge ACIP to once again serve as a source of credible, evidence-based information to inform vaccine coverage. Current members lacking vaccine expertise and appointed without transparency should be dismissed, and new members with the necessary qualifications should be appointed per the ACIP charter and federal law. Until ACIP is trustworthy and scientifically sound, it should not meet.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.
