Title: Comment from Prasad, Megan
Comment:
I was 28 years old and living in San Francisco when I received my first Pfizer COVID-19 vaccine on April 8, 2021. At that time, my life looked completely different from what it would soon become.
Before the vaccines, I was active and motivated. I worked out four to five days a week and ran regularly. I was focused on improving my health and had already begun losing weight despite living with PCOS. I had a goal of losing 50 pounds and felt like I was finally getting my life on track. I had no chronic pain, no mobility issues, and no serious physical health problems. My days were filled with work, exercise, and a sense of progress.
I received my second Pfizer dose on April 29, 2021. Not long after the first two shots, I began noticing pain in my lower back that I had never experienced before. At first I tried to ignore it. I assumed it was temporary or related to exercise. But the pain slowly intensified. Running became difficult. Walking started to hurt. Eventually even sitting for long periods became uncomfortable.
My workplace later required employees to receive a booster shot, despite the fact that I had already been experiencing troubling symptoms. I received my third Pfizer dose on January 11, 2022.
Over time, my health began to decline in ways I never could have imagined. Severe lower back pain became part of my daily life. My joints ached constantly, and I began suffering frequent headaches that made it hard to concentrate. Living with ADHD had always required extra focus and structure, but now the chronic pain and headaches made it extremely difficult to stay focused at work.
Then something even more alarming began to happen: multiple soft tumors started forming on different parts of my body. Some developed around my sacral joint and spine, which made movement even more painful and restricted my mobility. By December 2022, the tumors around my sacral joint had to be surgically removed.
Medical imaging began to show serious issues in my spine. Doctors found inflammation, disc problems, fluid buildup, and degeneration in my lower back. I was diagnosed with osteoarthritis, facet atrophy, and several other conditions that had never existed in my family history. In total, I was given more than eight new diagnoses within a short period of time.
The physical pain changed everything about my life. Activities that once brought me joy such as running, working out, socializing became impossible or exhausting. My social life disappeared because I often didn’t have the energy or mobility to go out. The weight I had been losing slowly returned as my physical activity dropped.
What made the experience even harder was the feeling that no one believed what I was going through. Many doctors dismissed my concerns or minimized my symptoms. I often felt like I was being gaslit while desperately searching for answers.
Meanwhile, work became increasingly difficult. Chronic pain made it hard to sit at my desk for long periods. Headaches disrupted my concentration. Medical appointments and flare-ups forced me to miss work more often than I ever had before.
Eventually my employer began treating my health struggles as a problem rather than a medical issue that required support. Despite the circumstances and my attempts to continue working, I was terminated from my job. From my perspective, it felt like I was being punished for health problems I could not control.
Losing my job was devastating. It wasn’t just the loss of income it was the loss of stability, independence, and dignity. I believed the termination was illegal because it was tied to my medical condition, so I decided to file a lawsuit in hopes of holding my employer accountable.
The legal process was long, stressful, and emotionally draining. Reliving my health struggles in court was painful, and I hoped that presenting the medical evidence and my story would lead to justice.
In the end, I lost the case.
That outcome left me feeling even more powerless. I had gone from being a healthy, active young woman to a chronic pain patient navigating multiple diagnoses, surgery, and uncertainty about my future. My career had been disrupted, my health permanently changed, and the system I hoped would help me instead left me feeling unheard.
Today I still live with the consequences of those years. Chronic pain affects my daily life, my ability to work, and my mental health. Some days are better than others, but the uncertainty remains.
What hurts the most is the feeling of isolation the sense that people don’t believe what happened to me or that there is little support for people who believe they were harmed and are trying to rebuild their lives.
My story is not just about illness. It is about loss: the loss of health, the loss of a career, and the loss of the life I was building before everything changed.

Title: Comment from Nenninger, Eileen
Comment:
As an infectious diseases medical professional, I urge ACIP to once again serve as a source of credible, evidence-based information to inform vaccine coverage. Current members lacking vaccine expertise and appointed without transparency should be dismissed, and new members with the necessary qualifications should be appointed per the ACIP charter and federal law. Until ACIP is trustworthy and scientifically sound, it should not meet.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

Title: Comment from Sullivan, Jill
Comment:
My name is Jill Sullivan. I am writing in response to Docket No. CDC-2026-0199.
I am a person living with vaccine injury. Before receiving the Moderna Covid-19 vaccine I was living a productive and happy life. However, within the first two weeks of the second dose, I began experiencing symptoms consistent with vaccine injury. I reported these to VAERS. Over the next 6 months, I dealt with severe chronic fatigue that significantly impaired my mobility, making basic tasks like walking short distances or climbing stairs a struggle. While the physicians I consulted acknowledged there was likely a link, they were not officially able to diagnose it as a C-19 vaccine reaction.
Through persistence, I have regained about 60% of my baseline health. Regardless, Covid and other viral infections trigger setbacks, so I have to keep working hard just to keep the progress I've made.
I have never experienced an issue with a non-MRNA vaccine. I feel vaccines are important, but for vaccines to maintain public trust, the institutions providing them must be fully transparent about risks and accountable for outcomes.
I urge the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices to consider perspectives from people like me that are living with vaccine injuries. Please consider how policies on vaccine recommendation guidelines, post-adverse event care, and research priorities affect real people every day.

Title: Comment from Rositas-Pistole, Andrea
Comment:
On 1/31/21 I had an immediate reaction to the Moderna vaccine which caused facial tingling, tachycardia, nausea and dizziness, and was found in the bathroom disorientated. Then on 2/12/21 I had a sharp pain in my neck through my chest and my heart started pounding. I called 911 since I never experienced anything like this before and thought it was a heart attack. My BP was 207/112 and HR 180 so they took me to the ER. My d-dimer was elevated so they did a CT scan which was clear. After this day it was like a switch went off in my body and I began experiencing many symptoms:
Symptoms starting 2/12/21:
* Started with tingling 24/7 in legs, then spread out to body
* Internal tremor/vibrations
* Muscle twitching
* Muscle weakness, mainly limbs upper thigh and arms/hands
* Fluctuation of blood pressure and heart rate- Improving, almost back to baseline
* Exercise intolerance- exertion causing an increase in symptoms and exhaustion.
* Fatigue, heavy body/head
* Head Pressure/ Headaches
* Eye blurriness
* Ringing in ears
* Palpitation sensation
* Adrenaline surges- feels like my body is malfunctioning my mind is fine, but my skin gets a goosebump feeling or dropping heart sensation
* Chest tightness
* Stabbing pain sensation and joint/muscle pain
* Episodes of dizziness, nausea, and lightheadedness. I check my BP, HR and blood sugar when this happens and it's normal.
* I have reactions to medication and food. After eating lots of stomach movement, with an increase in tingling, vibration, and head pressure. Also, an increase in IBS symptoms. I believe it's histamine issues but my 24hr test and tryptase was negative.

Diagnoses so far:
* Post-COVID-19 syndrome manifesting as chronic fatigue 
* Post-COVID-19 syndrome manifesting as chronic neurologic symptoms
* Dysautonomia
* Fibromyalgia
* Anxiety and Depression

Doctors are quick to dismiss my vaccine reaction and blame everything on the virus which I never caught until 7/2022.

I don’t live a full life. All these symptoms still impact my daily life everyday and every hour. I deserve answers and an explanation as to what happened to me. Why did this vaccine do this to me. When I signed the waiver to receive the Moderna Covid vaccine it said I would only have flu like symptoms. I wasn’t given transparency on all the reactions that are possible from a vaccine.

We don’t have vaccine injured specialists or a protocol for cases like mine. We need to start taking cases like mine seriously. I have emails from NIH and FDA from 2021 which showed they knew about these reactions but didn’t help us or acknowledge us.

Please fast track a treatment plan and access to care for vaccine injured.

Thank you, Andrea Rositas-Pistole

Title: Comment from Wagner, Jan
Comment:
I am responding to your request for comments on the Covid vaccine as a Grandparent who is raising a Grandchild per Generations United.
Due to our advanced ages with a youth still under our care it is extremely important that we do everything possible to remain as healthy as possible. Getting the covid vaccine is one thing we can do to protect ourselves, and for our Grand to protect us. Our close families and friends we are in frequent contact with are all vaccinated. Yes, some members have still gotten covid, but none of them have become seriously ill. Both my spouse and I have comorbidities that if we were not vaccinated, the virus would be very dangerous. I am on 2 different immunosupressive medications and I have also participated in 2 separate monoclonal antibody infusion studies as well as annual covid immunizations and boosters. Along with exercising caution when in public by wearing a mask and avoiding large indoor gatherings, I have never had covid this far! My husband, who has a heart condition, has had covid 3 times, without becoming seriously ill. He was able to take paxlovid the first two times, but due to a conflict with medication he was not able to take it most recently. Another medication, Legevro, is currently unavailable in our area within the five day window. Although he was not able to access the med, this case was still manageable because he has continued receiving the vaccine. The same for our Grand and family who have had the misfortune to contract the virus. There is no doubt to me the vaccine works and will hopefully continue to be available at no cost. Few people (including our little family) could afford to cover the cost out of pocket and many will go without.
Thank you for allowing to speak in support of the covid shot. A couple of days of soreness, fatigue, low grade temperature and headache are more than worth the benefit. I'd be more than willing to comment publicly if you need me to.

Title: Comment from Williams, Adrianna
Comment:
March 12, 2026

To the Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP),

I hereby formally demand that the ACIP immediately revise its policies to state that no vaccine shall be recommended for routine use in the general population unless it has undergone proper, large-scale, long-term, inert placebo-controlled trials (using a true saline placebo or equivalent inactive control, not another vaccine or adjuvant-containing substance) with full scrutiny of all safety outcomes, including all-cause mortality, serious adverse events, and long-term harms.

Recommendations without such gold-standard evidence render any endorsement scientifically invalid and ethically unacceptable. Approving products without definitive proof that they are not harmful—or worse, potentially poisonous—constitutes a grave failure of scientific duty and public trust.

Demands to ACIP and CDC:

1. Immediately suspend all routine recommendations for seasonal influenza vaccines (and any other vaccines) lacking large-scale, inert placebo-controlled safety data.
2. Issue a public statement acknowledging the absence of such trials for current influenza products and the resulting scientific uncertainty.
3. Require that future recommendations mandate inert placebo-controlled trials as a prerequisite, with full transparency of raw data, all-cause mortality, and all adverse events.
4. Prioritize independent re-evaluation of existing vaccines under these standards before reaffirming any use in healthy populations.

Failure to act risks further erosion of public confidence and potential harm. Science demands scrutiny, not blind endorsement.

This demand is submitted for the public record and ethical accountability.

Thank You.

Title: Comment from Barrera, Gloria E
Comment:
To the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices,

My name is Gloria E. Barrera. I am a registered nurse, certified school nurse, and nurse educator in Illinois. I serve as Director of the RN to BSN Program at the University of Illinois Chicago and continue to practice in school health. I am writing to share my support for strong, evidence based immunization policy and for the important role ACIP plays in protecting public health.

As a school nurse, I see every day how vaccines protect children, families, and communities. School nurses are often the first public health professionals families encounter. We help parents understand immunization requirements, answer questions, and prevent outbreaks in school settings. When vaccination rates decline or when confidence in vaccines is undermined, the impact is immediate in our schools and communities.

Vaccines remain one of the most effective public health tools we have. They prevent serious illness, reduce hospitalizations, and protect people who are most vulnerable, including infants, children with chronic conditions, and individuals with weakened immune systems. In school health, we see firsthand how immunization programs help keep students in the classroom and communities healthy.

I am concerned about the growing public confusion surrounding vaccines and the potential erosion of trust in the processes used to evaluate them. It is essential that decisions related to immunization policy continue to be grounded in strong scientific evidence and transparent processes. Public confidence in vaccines depends on the integrity of these systems.

Programs that expand access to vaccines are also critical for protecting the health of children and families. Many families rely on public health programs to ensure their children receive recommended immunizations. Maintaining strong immunization recommendations helps ensure that all children, regardless of income or circumstance, have access to lifesaving vaccines.

As a nurse, educator, and public health advocate, I urge ACIP to continue its commitment to science based decision making and to policies that protect the health of our communities. The work of this committee has real consequences for families, schools, and healthcare systems across the country.

Thank you for your continued work to safeguard public health.

Gloria E. Barrera, MSN, RN, PEL-CSN
Director, RN to BSN Program
University of Illinois Chicago
Certified School Nurse
Illinois

Title: Comment from Kotton, Camille
Comment:
As an infectious diseases medical professional and former voting member of ACIP (2020-2024), with decades of experience in the vaccination field, I strongly urge ACIP to once again serve as a source of credible, evidence-based information to inform vaccine coverage. Current members lacking vaccine expertise and appointed without transparency should be dismissed, and new members with the necessary qualifications should be appointed per the ACIP charter and federal law. Until ACIP is trustworthy and scientifically sound, it should not meet. Many many lives are at risk. The damage will not be easily reversible. Many of the Immunocompromised patients in my practice are at high risk for severe vaccine prevent preventable illness and need vaccine themselves, and also for their friends and loved ones to help prevent transmission.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity. Similar systems are used for much of our medical and scientific work.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object strongly to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

Title: Comment from Knudsen , Tine
Comment:
My name is TineKnudsen. I am writing in response to Docket No. CDC-2026-0199.
I am a person living with vaccine injury. I urge the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices to consider patient perspectives and real-world experiences in its deliberations. Our presence here reflects lived suffering, unmet needs, and the importance of inclusive dialogue. We do exist. Please consider how policies on vaccine recommendation guidelines, post-adverse event care, and research priorities affect real people every day.
#PatientVoices #ACIP #MakeYourVoiceHeard

Title: Comment from Vaccine Injured and Bereaved Jersey (VIBJSY)
Comment:
Dear ACIP Secretariat,

Please find attached the report I am submitting on behalf of individuals who have reported significant difficulties following Covid‑19 vaccination.

The experiences documented in this report reflect concerns that have been raised in many parts of the world, and the impact is clearly evident among those our support group works with in Jersey, Channel Islands. A recurring theme across these affected communities is the absence of formal recognition, the lack of clear and accessible support pathways, and limited availability of financial assistance.

I respectfully request that this material be included for consideration at the upcoming ACIP Meeting on 18 March 2026.

Kind regards,
Danielle Herve

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment: Thank you for taking the time to discuss this topic.  I am a pharmacist.  I am a professional in the medical community.  I support the use of vaccines and was a willing participant who not only vaccinated myself, but my children against COVID-19.  I am now vaccine injured.  It stole my health, my joy, my peace of mind, my faith in my profession and in the professional organizations that we put our trust in.  I was raised and trained to believe that when you were sick, if something happened to you, the medical system would help you.  That if there was anything to be worried about, we had systems in place to warn, to notify, and to help.  These systems failed too many of us.  I was told by emergency room physicians that it was just stress, as my blood pressure skyrocketed and I sat in a bed uncontrollably shaking.  I was told it was probably always my ‘normal’ when my labs went haywire.  Physicians wouldn’t believe what I was telling them, because “if COVID vaccines caused these problems, we would have heard about it.”  It is beyond disheartening when trained healthcare professionals would dismiss one of their own under the assumption that vaccines are 100% safe.  No drug is 100% safe.  Any medication, treatment, device or procedure has the potential to cause an unwanted outcome, however small that might be.  And to dismiss, gaslight and deny those impacted, however few they may be, is cruel.  We were made to sound like we had a typical, minor reactions that resolved, when years later myself and others still struggle with the ramifications of these vaccines.  I am ashamed to say, prior to getting injured myself, I was part of the problem.  I had patients tell me that the COVID vaccine “went to their brain” and now they were injured and suffering.  I didn’t believe them either, until it happened to me.  I assure you, as a patient and a pharmacist, these injuries are real.  They are devastating.  And we do exist.

Title: Comment from Shaw, Casey
Comment:
To whom it may concern regarding Docket No. CDC-2026-0199. I am a person living with a vaccine injury. I want to thank the CDC and the ACIP for taking the time to address the vaccine injured. I want to start by saying that I was excited to get the Pfizer Covid-19 vaccine. I studied a lot of the available information, watched videos of doctors describing MRNA vaccines and the difference of traditional vaccines, researched clinical trials and reviewed side effects and decided based on the available information, that getting this vaccine was going to be a low risk opportunity to protect myself and family and regain a sense of normalcy that I had been longing for since the pandemic began. This all changed after receiving the Pfizer booster, October of 2022.
I got the Pfizer Booster, October of 2022. The first two shots (the typical course) did not seem to cause any noticeable symptoms or concerns. Within five days of receiving the booster, my life changed presumably forever. I very quickly developed symptoms of "shocks" to my heart and a fast heart rate, lightheadedness, shortness of breath and chest pain. I also experienced what I refer to as "episodes" - a rush of adrenaline that includes presyncope, escalating heart rate, dizziness, sweaty palms, excessive thirst, irritability and whole-body tremors and loss of appetite. Additional symptoms include, but are not limited to, spine pain with leg weakness, brain fog, ongoing right side occipital pain with headaches, post-exertional malaise, etc. Important to note, these were all new symptoms, never experienced by me before the booster. During the remainder of 2022 and into 2023, I went to the ER five times, had three MRI’s, had numerous tests including seemingly countless blood tests, I was seen by multiple specialists including rheumatologist, neurologist, cardiologists and more.
In this year, 2026, I have come a very long way in my recovery, and I am grateful for that. However, I have been diagnosed with POTS and ME/CFS. I still have ongoing symptoms including everything with the POTS diagnosis which includes occasional presyncope followed by episodes, post-exertional malaise, spine pain, general pain and fatigue, right side occipital neck pain and headaches. These symptoms do come and go but are consistent enough I have had to resolve myself to the fact that these symptoms could be lifelong.
So in conclusion, I started out as someone who was excited to be a part of this new medical science and a citizen who wanted to help their family, friends and neighbors in fighting the pandemic. Now, I am someone who has trouble functioning in my daily life to the degree that I could before. I strongly encourage the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices and all involved to be strong advocates for those who have been injured, understanding that vaccines are like all medical modalities; they carry risks and people have been negatively impacted. I also hope that future, unbiased testing and research will be done on these vaccines to prevent future injury/pain and suffering.

Title: Comment from Barker, Jason
Comment:
As an infectious diseases medical professional, I urge ACIP to once again serve as a source of credible, evidence-based information to inform vaccine coverage. Current members lacking vaccine expertise and appointed without transparency should be dismissed, and new members with the necessary qualifications should be appointed per the ACIP charter and federal law. Until ACIP is trustworthy and scientifically sound, it should not meet.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

Title: Comment from Jaul, Dan
Comment: I have been a pediatrician for almost 40 years. A good chunk of my job has been the administering of vaccines. I applaud the study of long covid, and am hopeful that useful information will come of this study to help the hundreds of thousand of people suffering with this disease. Having cut my teeth during my residency watching newborns die by the hundreds of Pertussis until the Pertussis vaccine came into widespread use, I have seen firsthand that vaccines are among the greatest achievements of mankind. The tragedy unfolding in South Carolina and other states regarding measles are small but concrete examples of this. Denying the utility of vaccines would be no more productive than denying that gravity exists, or that oxygen is useful for living things. There are thousand of anecdotes of side effects from vaccines. In my practice I've treated vaccine side effects commonly. Fevers and pain at the injection sites are by far the most common side effects. More serious side effects are quite uncommon and generally short-lived and transient. They pale in comparison to the side effects of antibiotics and other common medications. Even the exceedingly rare dire side effects of vaccines do not come close to negating the morbidity and mortality of the diseases that vaccines prevent or modify, or the hundreds of millions of lives these vaccines have saved. I sincerely hope our government uses real science for the benefit and health of US citizens, not as a political tool.

Title: Comment from Connecticut Department of Public Health
Comment:
ACIP Secretariat, ACIP Meeting
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
1600 Clifton Road NE, Mailstop H21-12
Atlanta, Georgia 30329-4027

Attn: Docket No. CDC-2026-0199
March 12, 2026

Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices,

The Connecticut Department of Public Health (CT DPH) appreciates the opportunity to submit written comments in advance of the March 18–19, 2026 ACIP meeting. We write to urge the Committee to preserve the integrity of its established evidence-based processes for developing vaccine recommendations and to safeguard the independence of the Committee itself. These are foundational to public trust in immunization policy. Connecticut continues to experience the burden of vaccine-preventable illness underscoring that ACIP’s work has direct, measurable consequences for the health of our residents.

CT DPH strongly encourages ACIP to reaffirm and consistently apply its established recommendation methodology, including the Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development, and Evaluation (GRADE) methodology and the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework. These tools provide a structured, transparent, and reproducible approach to evaluating vaccine safety, effectiveness, feasibility, and equity—and represent the gold standard for translating scientific evidence into actionable public health guidance. Any deviation from this framework, whether by circumventing the evidence review process, compressing deliberation timelines, or bypassing workgroup analysis, would fundamentally undermine the legitimacy of ACIP’s recommendations. Given that ACIP recommendations shape clinical practice, insurance coverage under the Affordable Care Act, and eligibility under the Vaccines for Children program, they must be grounded in a rigorous and replicable process. CT DPH urges the Committee to resist any pressure to depart from the standards that have made ACIP one of the most trusted scientific advisory bodies in the world.

Equally important is the independence and composition of the Committee itself. ACIP’s credibility rests on the expertise and independence of its members, selected through a transparent process based on scientific qualifications. CT DPH urges that the nomination, selection, and retention of ACIP members continue to prioritize scientific expertise free from political influence. The removal or replacement of members for reasons unrelated to their qualifications, or appointments driven by political rather than scientific considerations, would compromise the integrity of the Committee and the recommendations it produces. When advisory bodies are perceived as subject to political influence, public trust erodes—leading to declining vaccination rates, resurgence of preventable disease, and greater susceptibility to health misinformation. CT DPH urges ACIP to recognize that its commitment to methodological rigor and institutional independence is not merely a procedural preference, it is a public health imperative.

CT DPH remains committed to advancing evidence-based immunization policy that protects all Connecticut residents. We respectfully urge the Committee to uphold the standards of scientific integrity, methodological rigor, and institutional independence that are essential to effective public health practice.

Thank you for the opportunity to provide comments.

Sincerely,

Manisha Juthani, MD
Commissioner
Connecticut Department of Public Health

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment:
I serve as the Executive Director of a nonprofit organization dedicated to improving immunization rates through education, advocacy, and partnerships with healthcare providers and communities across our state.

I am submitting this comment as both a public health professional and a mother.

My son is now 23 years old and lives with type 1 diabetes. Like many individuals with chronic conditions, he remains at higher risk for complications from vaccine-preventable diseases. Throughout his childhood, the routine immunization schedule provided a clear, science-based roadmap that helped protect him during years when his immune system and overall health were particularly vulnerable.

For families like mine, the childhood immunization schedule is more than a list of recommendations—it is a trusted framework built on decades of rigorous scientific evidence, disease surveillance, and careful evaluation of safety and effectiveness. Parents, clinicians, and public health professionals rely on ACIP’s recommendations because they represent the gold standard of evidence-based medicine.

Any changes that weaken or create confusion around the routine childhood immunization schedule have real consequences. We are already seeing the impact of declining vaccination coverage in outbreaks of measles, pertussis, and other preventable diseases across the United States. For individuals with chronic conditions such as diabetes, asthma, or immune compromise, these diseases are not minor inconveniences—they can lead to severe illness, hospitalization, and lifelong complications.

The childhood immunization schedule has been one of the most successful public health tools in modern history. Vaccines have prevented millions of hospitalizations and deaths in the United States alone. Maintaining a clear, consistent, and evidence-based schedule is critical not only for individual protection but also for community immunity that protects those who are most vulnerable.

I urge the committee to continue grounding its recommendations firmly in the scientific evidence and to protect the integrity of the childhood immunization schedule that families, providers, and public health programs rely upon every day.

As both a public health professional and the parent of a child with a chronic health condition, I know firsthand how important these recommendations are. Families like mine depend on the work of this committee to ensure that the strongest possible protections remain in place for all children.

Title: Comment from Warren, Pamela
Comment:
My name is Pam. I am writing in response to Docket No. CDC-2026-0199.
I am suffering from a severe vaccine side effect of the covid vaccine. I have documented proof of my post vaccine injury through my Rheumatologist. He has reported this to the FDA and there has been no follow up, no help offered and no public statements from those of us suffering. I urge the advisory committee on Immunization Practices to consider patients in this group. There are 1000's of us that have been completey ignored. I do not want, I do not want revenge, I want recognition. Many of us have struggled on our own to be heard and validated by the government agencies that forced this on us.I was a person that happily agreed to get vaccinated and believed it would save me and others. Instead I was injured...please hear me/us. Please let me speak to anyone that will listen to us.
Please consider how policies on vaccine recommendation guidelines, post adverse event care, and research priorities affect real people every day.

Pam Warren

Title: Comment from Bailey, Emily
Comment:
Hi,
I am a mom, a Kansan, and someone who thinks vaccines are really cool. I love that vaccines protect my family, especially my two young children, from diseases that used to be significant threats to our lives and quality of life.
Truly, vaccinations remain one of the greatest achievements in public health. They have dramatically
reduced the spread of dangerous diseases such as measles, polio, and mumps and continue to
protect the health of Americans. Vaccines are safe, effective, and continually monitored to ensure
their safety and efficacy.
As a parent of a preschool daughter and a toddler son, vaccines are also personal for me. I want
to know that when my children attend preschool, visit the doctor, or participate in community
activities, the people around them are protected against preventable diseases. Vaccination helps
keep our communities safe for young children who are still developing their immune systems.
Strong vaccination protections are also important for keeping schools, childcare centers, and
healthcare settings safe—especially in rural communities where access to healthcare may already
be limited and outbreaks can place additional strain on local systems.
We have already seen the consequences when vaccination coverage declines. The measles
outbreak last year cost my state more than $2.6 million between January 1 and June 16, 2025,
and the majority of cases occurred among unvaccinated individuals. ACIP should carefully
consider whether it is willing to risk perpetuating expensive and preventable public
health crises across our nation with non-scientific recommendations that erode public trust in and decrease uptake of vaccinations.
Please see the attached infographic detailing the anticipated impact of of changes to the childhood immunization schedule without newly presented evidence. Ultimately, this path leads to a sicker country, strained healthcare systems, and higher costs for all of us. My friends who are nurses and doctors are already feeling the impacts of these baseless changes to the immunization schedule as more parents choose not to vaccinate their children leaving them, and others in the community, much more vulnerable to vaccine-preventable diseases. Vaccine-preventable diseases that we already see coming back as a result of decreased vaccination rates.
You have an incredible responsibility as ACIP to be leaders in making the very best vaccination recommendations to protect the health of Americans. Please support scientific, clinical, evidence-based vaccine recommendations. Thank you.

Title: Comment from Williams, Breyana
Comment:
My name is Breyana Williams, and I am a community member who cares deeply about protecting public health and ensuring that people have access to the tools they need to stay healthy. Vaccination is one of those tools, and it matters to me because I believe everyone deserves the ability to make informed decisions to protect themselves and the people around them.

Adults deserve the right to protect themselves from serious illness. Even if someone personally believes their own risk is low, that decision doesn’t exist in isolation. Many people in our communities — including older adults, people living with cancer, transplant recipients, and individuals with disabilities — face a much higher risk of severe illness. Vaccination helps reduce those risks and helps protect the people who may be most vulnerable.

What matters most to me is that decisions continue to be guided by evidence and transparency. We should keep carefully monitoring vaccine safety, openly sharing data with the public, and updating recommendations as new information becomes available. When people see that safety is being tracked and information is shared honestly, it builds trust and helps people make informed choices.

At its core, this issue is about fairness and access. Everyone should have the option to use proven preventive tools to protect their health and the health of their families. Limiting that option could leave vulnerable people with fewer ways to stay safe.

For these reasons, I respectfully ask ACIP to continue supporting access to vaccination for adults and to ensure that recommendations remain grounded in strong evidence, transparency, and the goal of protecting those at greatest risk.

Thank you for your time and consideration.

Title: Comment from Cunha, Corinne
Comment:

March 12, 2026

Title: Comment from Remark, S
Comment:
As an internal medicine physician in community practice in Wisconsin since 1989, I am writing to express my concern about the direction of ACIP since June of 2025. Given the current inability of the ACIP to use science-based evidence and processes in decision-making, I urge postponement of the upcoming ACIP meeting. Prior to June 2025, the strong foundation of evidence-based policy-making by ACIP was crucial to doctors in practice such as myself. The changes in the members of the ACIP committee, the gutting of CDC processes and support for ACIP practices and the biased and conflicted decisions of the committee are threatening the ability of healthcare in the U.S. to give good vaccine advice to patients.
Of hundreds of examples in my 37 years in practice, here are a few stories that stand out:
The 92 year old patient who accepted his Tetanus-diptheria vaccine gratefully, telling me "Thank God for this vaccine. My father died of diphtheria when I was a child."
The dozens of elderly patients I have cared for who were/are hobbled by polio contracted as a child.
The gratitude of healthcare workers lining up to receive their first COVID vaccines, recognizing the light at the end of an incredibly dark tunnel after watching the ravages of COVID-19 on our patients and colleagues. Operation Warp Speed was a marvel of American know-how and ingenuity, traits we want to build up.
Thank you for your attention.
Marianne Remark, MD
Madison, WI

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment:
I am submitting this comment as a family caregiver for someone living with a serious neurological injury that occurred shortly after COVID-19 vaccination. I recognize that vaccines play an important role in public health, but it is essential that public policy also respect individual medical choice and acknowledge that medical interventions carry risks for some people.

No individual should feel coerced into receiving a medical treatment without the ability to make a fully informed decision based on their personal health circumstances. Respect for informed consent and freedom of medical choice is a cornerstone of ethical medicine.

There should be clear pathways for recognition, treatment, research, and support.

I respectfully urge the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices to ensure that vaccine policy continues to prioritize informed consent, transparency about potential risks, and meaningful care for those who experience adverse events.

Thank you for considering the perspectives of affected patients and families.

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment: The covid vaccine killed one of my best friends. Her symptoms were 1st very painful legs & a couple of years later, extremely aggressive cancer in lungs and bone. She had had every one of the covid shots.

Title: Comment from Snow, Robert
Comment: My name is Robert Snow, and I am writing in response to Docket No. CDC-2026-0199. I am a person with a vaccine injury and I am an anomaly as statistics say I should be dead. Until April 9, 2022, I was a professional airline pilot. On that date I collapsed due to cardiac arrest in the cockpit of a commercial airliner I had just piloted and parked at the gate. I likely would not have survived the experience were it not for quick intervention by medically qualified passengers and the coincidental close proximity of professional EMTs. After CPR and application of an AED, I spent 6 days in the ICU prior to discharge and am now unable to pursue my career as I can no longer hold the FAA aeromedical certificate necessary to exercise the privileges of my Airline Transport Pilot's license. Despite having previously had Covid-19 and testing positive for antibodies, under the terms of Presidential EO 14042 (subsequently injuncted after I received my injection) I was forced by my employer to receive an EUA Covid-19 "vaccine" under threat of termination. Due to my concerns regarding mRNA technology, I elected to receive the J&J/Janssen product in order to preserve my job and provide for my family. A few weeks after receiving the injection I began to experience symptoms that I now realize were likely cardiac in nature. At the time I had no reason to suspect cardiac damage as I was a fit 60-yo and had been receiving regular EKGs per standard FAA medical requirements in addition to routine examinations by an interventional cardiologist due to previously diagnosed mild hypertension. All previous testing protocol results were normal and there was nothing to indicate I was a candidate for sudden cardiac arrest as I was healthy and there is no such history in my family. All extensive post-SCA tests, including genetic, further reinforce that position. Ultimately, the incredible irony is that I lost my career and my quality of life in an attempt to maintain them, all under duress. I was almost killed by an ill-conceived, reckless protocol too eagerly embraced by a medical profession that was once the envy of the world; others did lose their lives or were disabled by their injections and we have yet to ascertain the full extent of the long term harm. I urge the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices to recommend significant, substantive action to evaluate and change vaccine recommendation guidelines to include EUA products, informed consent practices (I received none), post adverse event care standards and research priorities to help restore trust in a system that I, and countless others, now consider thoroughly compromised and highly suspect. We exist, and we are watching closely.

Title: Comment from Finley, Bridgett
Comment:
As an infectious diseases medical professional, I urge ACIP to once again serve as a source of credible, evidence-based information to inform vaccine coverage. Current members lacking vaccine expertise and appointed without transparency should be dismissed, and new members with the necessary qualifications should be appointed per the ACIP charter and federal law. Until ACIP is trustworthy and scientifically sound, it should not meet.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

Title: Comment from Parikka, Hanna
Comment:
Docket number CDC-2026-0199

My name is Hanna Parikka. I’m writing to you as a former academic reproductive health professional with 20 years’ experience as an IVF biologist (equivalent to embryologist) in Finland. Since the gene-based Covid vaccines rollout, I have had extreme concerns about the lack of safety data, pharmacovigilance, transparency, precautionary principle, accountability, informed consent, professional ethics & competence as well as free speech in scientific and ethical issues in our society and amongst us professionals.

We were told by the authorities that the safety signals have been closely monitored and nothing of concern has been found. I would like to ask: who is monitoring, where are the written guidelines and who is responsible for the collected data? I know as a professional that concerning the Covid vaccines, there were no instructions or requirements for traceability from the authorities.

The IVF field could have been the perfect early-warning system - why wasn't it implemented?

This is not a question of IVF, but possible safety signals which could be seen especially in the IVF laboratory, and which possibly could explain the decline in global fertility & birth rates. IVF field could have been able to detect some biological disturbances long before they become visible as reduced fertility outcomes. Known risks associated with gene-based Covid vaccines (e.g. lipid nanoparticles, spike protein, modified mRNA, residual DNA, SV40 promoter/enhancer sequences) may also affect fertility at a very early stage, which can lead to problems with cell or early embryo quality, implantation issues, or very early miscarriage. Also, we cannot think in a way that we aren’t seeing any safety signal if the child is born alive. IVF outcome tracking should have been mandatory from 2021 onward. Failing to do so was not only a complete scientific failure, but especially a moral failure born of haste, political pressure and institutional capture.

Since the introduction of Covid vaccines some professionals (me included) in the IVF field have shared their concerning observations in the IVF clinics, e.g. less mature oocytes (MII), degenerating oocytes and embryos, lower blastocyst formation, impaired quality of eggs, sperm and embryos, unusual findings in PGT (pre-implantation genetic testing), implantation failures, increased miscarriages and impaired sperm quality. The fundamental problem is that there are no requirements or guidelines provided by the authorities for the monitoring of safety signals, and the information of patients’ vaccination status is not provided – this makes it very difficult to find any safety signals linked to the gene-based Covid vaccines in the IVF lab. Thus, you won’t probably find anything.

The data collected by authorities from IVF clinics is far too generalized, and detecting safety signals in these datasets may be impossible or delayed by years. We need much more detailed data from IVF clinics to really find any safety signal sooner than decades too late. The parameters in the IVF laboratory that should be compared before and after the introduction of Covid vaccines are e.g. mature eggs (MII %), degenerated / weak eggs, egg survival after ICSI, normal fertilization (2 PN), fragmentation, timing delays in embryo cleavage, multinucleation, blastocyst formation rate, high quality blastocysts, re expansion after freezing, early cleavage arrest, sperm quality, sperm DNA fragmentation, PGT genetic testing results (aneuploid / mosaic /chaotic embryos) - this is not a complete list. We must also make use of all possible independent knowledge and currently developed test methods that can detect Covid vaccine derived disruptions in e.g. gametes, embryos, gonads, endometrium, placenta, foetus and newborn child. At this time, at the latest, no conflicts of interest must interfere with these investigations.

The only ethical step forward now is independent retrospective analysis: combining national reproductive health registries, IVF clinic data, miscarriage statistics, ovarian reserve data and gamete bank data – and making anonymous data fully available to the public.

IVF professionals make daily observations in their work, which is why it is completely absurd to close your eyes in your own laboratory as if there is no right and obligation to apply your knowledge and experience - and instead, without examining conflicts of interest, read in a scientific publication that "no safety signal has emerged in our large dataset". Professional ethics and moral responsibility have been made impossible if the only permissible way to carry out one's work is to blindly follow the instructions coming from above. As professionals, we must all act in a manner that makes us worthy of the trust of our patients, children, young people, and future generations.

Sincerely, Hanna Parikka, MSc, fmr IVF biologist

Title: Comment from National Minority Quality Forum
Comment:
See attached file(s)
The National Minority Quality Forum (NMQF) is a 501(c)(3) not-for-profit research and
advocacy organization based in Washington, DC. The mission of NMQF is to reduce patient risk by assuring optimal care for all. Our vision is an American health services research, delivery and financing system whose operating principle is to reduce patient risk for amenable morbidity and mortality while improving quality of life.

For more than 25 years, NMQF has worked to ensure that health services policy and financing eliminate rather than reinforce or exacerbate the gaps in healthcare quality and access that can be experienced by all patient cohorts and communities. NMQF believes that healthcare policies should be designed to deliver direct, observable benefits that enable accountability and adhere to principles that align resource allocation with biological realities and population health needs. The converse -- healthcare policy that operates without a patient-centered organizing principle -- redounds by oversight or intention to economic optimization and administrative convenience, frameworks that oblige their adherents to rationalize inevitable individual patient risk as cost considerations override therapeutic imperatives. Consistent with our Physical Laws Framework (PLF), therefore, NMQF supports policy and regulatory constructs which serve to establish patient risk reduction as the fundamental metric for healthcare policy evaluation.

The established American consensus regarding employing vaccines to manage infectious diseases has been broken, and the subsequent avoidable illness and death is made manifest daily. Vaccine Administration is a communal act. America’s families and communities, in partnership with our elected and appointed leaders, cannot permit retrogression to an unenlightened society that sacrifices the lives and the futures of the innocent or disenfranchised.

NMQF encourages ACIP, during your deliberations regarding your recommendation methodology, to reinforce your commitment to our societal obligation to quell the spread of vaccine-preventable diseases. The National Minority Quality Forum would welcome an opportunity to continue this essential discussion with the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices.

Questions regarding this comment should be directed to Gretchen C. Wartman, NMQF Vice President for Policy and Program (gwartman@nmqf.org).

Most people don't know that the product that Pfizer studied in the trial, and the product they released are two different products— borne of two entirely different manufacturing processes.

Process 2 vaccine—the one that was delivered to the public— is contaminated with billions of particles of DNA per vial. These "Process 2" vaccines also had substantially lower mRNA integrity. Regulators missed those critical facts, which is an epic failure.

What I've learned is that people have one of three responses once they find out. Either, "I don't believe that" or, "I don't care" or "OMG, the product was never tested, and so never should've been approved. It should be taken off the market."

252 people got the actual Process 2 product in the trial (for their second dose), and that is not a number or approach that yields valid conclusions.
People should care.

"Oh well, they never actually had a clinical trial on the real product. No matter," is what most of the world has settled on. But it is a huge matter.

Profound vaccine injuries experienced by so many of my friends continue to cause massive suffering. I suffer as well, from an immediate and (so far) permanent neurological vaccine injury. Title: Comment from Peck, Danielle
Comment:
The ACIP should make COVID vaccine recommendations specific to vendors and platforms, particularly in support of Nuvaxovid.

There are many benefits to Nuvaxovid/Novavax: broadened antigenic range, durability, fewer side effects, and longer lasting protection.

Nuvaxovid/Novavax should also be approved for a pediatric population. Children of all ages need more vaccine choices. Right now, Novavax is only available to patients 12 and over - we need much broader access.

We must also ensure that COVID vaccines are available to everyone with no out-of-pocket costs and that vaccines are available as early as possible. There is almost always a summer wave of COVID, and it tends to coincide with school beginning in August and September. Having vaccines available ahead of this will promote health and safety in the population.

Title: Comment from Levins, Elizabeth
Comment:
I urge ACIP to make recommendations/approvals for pediatric use of the Nuvaxovid vaccinations. mRNA vaccines cannot be the only option for pediatrics, especially when there are concerns with long term exposure to mRNA technology. The Nuvaxovid vaccine has broader antigenic coverage, longer durability and has fewer side effects than mRNA. This should be the preferred SARS-CoV-2 vaccine for all patients (pediatrics and adults), and it currently lacking pediatric approval. This is impacting availability at pharmacies, pediatrician offices and clinics. This leads parents to have to make the difficult decision to give their child a less effective vaccine- especially for parents that have children <2 years old who are unable to mask. A vaccine with broader protection and lasting durability in a child who has very few other ways to prevent infection with SARS-CoV-2 is critical in preventing infections and development of Long Covid.

I urge ACIP to also make recommendations on a biannual SARS-CoV-2 vaccination schedule. Children across the country continue to experience infections during the summer and early fall due to current recommendation for only annual vaccination. Children need to be protected when they go back to school, and waiting until a late fall/winter for vaccination is proving to be inadequate and harmful.

Title: Comment from Anonymous
Comment:
Our son has been vaccine injured for over 16 years. We allowed the pediatrician to give him all the shots she wanted to. I remember signing papers in the pediatrician’s office, giving them permission to give my son a shot. Little did I know that I was not signing for only 1 shot, but a series of 5 shots! I didn’t realize that until I took a look at his immunization record. That record has a “Reaction” column for every shot. The pediatrician left that column BLANK for every shot. Our son had many reactions to the shots over the years. When he was 2 years old, after a shot he had trouble breathing to the point where he almost died! But, the pediatrician put it down as him having asthma, which he never had. He also had unexplained rashes and unexplained low-grade fevers that would last for months.
He got 5 SHOTS in 1 DAY when he was only 2 MONTHS OLD, and almost every 2 months after that. His mother took him to most of the “well visits” during which he got the shots. Since he was a baby, she would bring him home from the visits and he would be sleeping. His mother would tell me every time that he got the shots plus Tylenol to ease the pain of the shots. Yet, that combination knocked him out. You should leave a doctor’s office feeling more healthy and energetic, not fatigued.
Our son fought the good fight against the side effects of the shots. Until he was between 3 and 4 years old, he was learning words and imitating phrases. Then, he lost all speech. Whatever shots he had around that time were the final blows. Now, he is 19 years old and cannot speak a clear word or sentence. He cannot read, write or do math. He started having seizures when he was about 4 years old and is now on 4 drugs to control them. He is also on 2 other drugs to address what they call “autism”, but what I call VACCINE INJURY!
As a result, our son is unable to get up in the morning to make it outside. I have to stay inside with him and watch as nice days pass outside until the sun goes down. We were stuck inside, good weather or bad way before the government started lockdowns in 2020. They made no difference to us whatsoever.
We take a lot of harassment from the local school district, who think we keep him home on purpose. We followed their advice when he was little, getting him dressed in bed and forcing him onto a bus. He would come home with a bag of wet clothes and some projects that were done by the teacher-aids who put our son’s name on them. Each year he would learn nothing, and they would have a big celebration for themselves of completing a school year. In later school years, I would drop him off at school, then get reports (from a more honest teacher) that our son would lay down in the hallways while the teacher was trying to get him to class.
What hurt the school situation the most was the lockdowns. The school system has not recovered from that. Communications went online and totally exclude our son because he has no computer skills. He has every right to attend his meetings yet they are made inaccessible to him.
The lockdowns hurt our son the most because they closed all the playgrounds, disabling all the swings and other equipment. That was the only outing he ever had. Then, because all indoor activities were cancelled, fully-abled adults and kids took over the parks making it more difficult for our son to get any space or fresh air.
I knew the childhood vaccines did more harm than good for our son, that’s why, thanks be to the almighty God, I kept my family away from the fake “covid vaccines”. We were able to find a doctor who gave us treatment that helped us get over the virus, although the state we live in tried to stop it, claiming that there was no treatment. We have family and friends who either still got covid, or are sick or died from blood clots, cancer, paralysis and other things after getting that shot.
Our son needs extra therapy and more space because of his hyperactivity. The places they call “schools” are overwhelmed, understaffed and crowded. Disabled children are put together with juvenile delinquents, putting them in a dangerous situation. We need to move to a house because our son needs more space and also because there are 6 WIRELESS UTILITY METERS on the other side of the wall of HIS BEDROOM! No doctor can tell us whether they cause harm or not because there are no published studies about them. The only agency that investigated is the FCC. However, the FCC is only concerned as to whether an electronic device interferes with other communication equipment. They have no interest as to how the device hurts people.
I was looking into buying a house at the beginning of 2020, but then the lockdowns happened. Real estate became almost impossible to buy for a middle-class family. It seems like it was planned that way, and I would not be surprised to find that it was.
Corporate greed is what is harming our son; vaccines, utility meters, and corporations buying up houses.

Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Title: Comment from Carroll, Shanna
Comment:
My name is Shanna Carroll, and I am the mother of Aubrynn.

Aubrynn was 17 years old. She was bright, kind, and deeply loved. She was an old soul who cared deeply for others and loved with her whole heart.

In the summer of 2022, Aubrynn was selected for a school trip scheduled from July 9 to July 22. In order to attend, the COVID vaccine was required. Our family had serious concerns and we pleaded with her not to receive it, but she felt pressure because it was mandatory for the trip. She was the only member of our family who received the vaccine. Aubrynn received her first dose on June 7, 2022, and her second dose on June 28, 2022.

She left for her trip excited and full of life.

On July 18, Aubrynn texted me saying she was not feeling well. Without hesitation, my husband and I drove from Michigan to New York to bring her home. We arrived the following day, which happened to be our three year old’s birthday. We celebrated quietly with cake and ice cream. Later that night, Aubrynn and I shared a bowl of soup before she went to bed.

The next morning, I took her to urgent care. We waited for hours. At one point, she slowly sat up and softly asked me, “Did they call me yet?”

Moments later, before we even understood what was happening, my daughter collapsed into cardiac arrest.

She was airlifted to a children’s hospital and placed on an ECMO machine to keep her alive. She was also treated with Remdesivir. Over the next days, her body began shutting down. Her limbs turned black from lack of blood flow. Infections spread throughout her body. Her kidneys failed.

On August 6, we were told there was nothing more the doctors could do.

We were forced to make the most devastating decision any parent could face. We had to turn off the machines and say goodbye to our daughter.

Aubrynn was not just a number. She was not just a statistic.

She was my child.

She had dreams, a future, and a family who loved her more than words can express.

I am sharing Aubrynn’s story because her life mattered. Her story matters. And I will spend the rest of my life speaking her name and telling the truth about what happened to her.

Title: Comment from Hudson, Heather
Comment:
A Mother’s Account: Fighting for My Son
On Sunday, March 1, 2026, my 26 year old son suffered his fifth stroke. I am writing this at his hospital bedside while doctors try to stabilize him. During this hospitalization they discovered a blood clot in his heart along with noninfectious endocarditis, an inflammatory heart disorder. Their hope is to perform open heart surgery to remove the clot, but right now he is too sick to survive the operation. His autoimmune blood clotting disorder continues to form clots even while he is fully anticoagulated.
This most recent stroke has been devastating. It damaged a large portion of the right side of his brain. His left arm is now immobile, and he has been mostly non verbal since the stroke. My son, who once spoke easily and loved life, now has to relearn how to speak.
Even as he is hospitalized this week, despite his blood thinners, he has developed more clots, one in his arm and deep vein thromboses in both legs at the same time. Doctors now consider his condition terminal.
Watching this happen to your child is something no mother should ever have to experience.
Before 2021, my son was healthy, strong, and full of promise. When he was 21, he was an avid runner. He ran twenty five miles a week at the gym and loved running along the seashore. He was not under a doctor’s care. He had been tested for autoimmune diseases twice before the pandemic and had none. He had a bright future ahead of him.
After receiving a COVID-19 vaccine late July 2021, everything changed.
In the months that followed, my son developed a severe autoimmune blood-clotting disorder called antiphospholipid syndrome (APS). He also survived catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome CAPS the most severe and life threatening form of the disease. From that point forward, his body began attacking itself.
He was hospitalized repeatedly with life threatening complications. He suffered a severe pulmonary embolism, developed multiple blood clots in both lungs, and a deep vein thrombosis in his right leg. Imaging later showed he had also suffered a stroke. His heart became enlarged and his valves began leaking.
After those early strokes, my once active son was left with profound weakness on his right side and could no longer walk on his own.
Then the autoimmune problems continued to multiply. He was diagnosed with Raynaud’s syndrome, Sjögren’s syndrome, and a hemorrhagic lung disorder. Neurological conditions appeared as well, including postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome POTS and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy CIDP. Most of these conditions developed rapidly within days or months after the vaccine.
In 2022 and 2023 he returned to college and was a 4.0 student. I watched him push through illness that would stop most people. But this disease kept progressing.
Between October 2023 and March 2024, he suffered three more strokes. He developed a blood clot in the cerebral venous system and severe narrowing of the veins in his brain. He was hospitalized again with deep vein thromboses occurring simultaneously in all four limbs.
From the beginning of this illness, his mobility was severely limited. On a good day he could walk about fifty feet with a cane before needing to stop or use a wheelchair.
For more than four years, I have fought for my son.
We have gone from hospital to hospital searching for help and turned away or dismissed repeatedly. My son was never placed on immune-modulating therapies or monoclonal antibody treatments that might address the autoimmune nature of his disease.
Because this vaccine injury is not recognized, hospitals often do not follow up properly. The medical system keeps asking for proof. Without recognition there is no pathway to gather it.
For years doctors have known he has antiphospholipid syndrome. Yet clear treatment protocols never materialized. Insurance barriers blocked care. Disability systems blocked access. In many cases doctors simply did not pursue treatment at all.
The long struggle to get someone, anyone, to help has now led to this catastrophic moment.
My son’s fifth stroke has taken a large part of the left side of his brain. His arm is paralyzed. He is fighting to speak again. And the blood clotting disorder causing it was never properly addressed.
As a mother, there is nothing more painful than watching your child suffer and feeling powerless to help them. I would do anything to save my son. I have spent years fighting for answers, begging doctors to look deeper, asking systems to recognize what is happening to him.
No mother should have to watch her child’s life slip away like this while the system argues about whether the problem even exists.
That is why I am asking the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) to recognize severe COVID-19 vaccine injuries in young people like my son.
The first step to solving a problem is acknowledging that it exists.
You cannot find what you are not looking for.
My son needs help.
